--- SOURCE 1: Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large ... ---
URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1

SUMMARY:
We introduce Conceptual Metaphor Theory (CMT) as a framework for enhancing large language models (LLMs) through cognitive prompting in complex reasoning tasks. To evaluate this approach, we compare four native models (Llama3.2, Phi3, Gemma2, and Mistral) against their CMT-augmented counterparts on benchmark tasks spanning domain-specific reasoning, creative insight, and metaphor interpretation. Experimental results indicate that CMT prompting significantly enhances reasoning accuracy, clarity, and metaphorical coherence, outperforming baseline models across all evaluated tasks. These mappings, integrated as CMT-prompts in an LLM-configuration process, enable the models to internalize metaphorical reasoning and apply it dynamically across tasks. By embedding these examples in the CMT-prompts, the models acquire the ability to generalize structured metaphorical reasoning across a wide range of abstract and domain-specific tasks, enhancing their interpretive and explanatory capabilities.

FULL CONTENT:
[Back to arXiv](https://arxiv.org/)

[License: arXiv.org perpetual non-exclusive license](https://info.arxiv.org/help/license/index.html#licenses-available)

arXiv:2502.01901v1 [cs.CL] 04 Feb 2025

# Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models

Oliver Kramer

Computational Intelligence Group
  
Department of Computer Science
  
Universität Oldenburg, Germany
  
Email: oliver.kramer@uni-oldenburg.de

###### Abstract

We introduce Conceptual Metaphor Theory (CMT) as a framework for enhancing large language models (LLMs) through cognitive prompting in complex reasoning tasks. CMT leverages metaphorical mappings to structure abstract reasoning, improving models’ ability to process and explain intricate concepts. By incorporating CMT-based prompts, we guide LLMs toward more structured and human-like reasoning patterns.
To evaluate this approach, we compare four native models (Llama3.2, Phi3, Gemma2, and Mistral) against their CMT-augmented counterparts on benchmark tasks spanning domain-specific reasoning, creative insight, and metaphor interpretation. Responses were automatically evaluated using the Llama3.3 70B model. Experimental results indicate that CMT prompting significantly enhances reasoning accuracy, clarity, and metaphorical coherence, outperforming baseline models across all evaluated tasks.

###### Index Terms:

Conceptual Metaphor Theory, Large Language Models, Prompt Engineering, Benchmark Evaluation, Complex Reasoning

## I Introduction

Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in various domains, including natural language understanding and complex problem-solving. However, enhancing their reasoning capabilities across diverse tasks remains challenging. Integrating insights from Conceptual Metaphor Theory (CMT), which emphasizes understanding abstract concepts through metaphorical mappings from concrete domains, offers a promising approach. By designing prompts to incorporate metaphoric reasoning, LLMs can be guided to tackle complex reasoning tasks more effectively.

In this study, we propose a CMT-inspired prompt engineering strategy to enhance the reasoning capabilities of LLMs. We hypothesize that LLMs can generate more coherent, contextually rich, and insightful responses across diverse task categories using CMT-inspired prompts.
We evaluate this approach on a benchmark dataset containing 100 tasks from multiple reasoning domains, including domain-specific reasoning, creative insight puzzles, and general problem-solving. Using four advanced LLMs – Llama3.2, Phi3, Gemma2, and Mistral – we rigorously assess the effectiveness of CMT-inspired prompts compared to baseline prompts in terms of accuracy, coherence, and depth of reasoning.

The findings from this study highlight the potential of metaphor-guided prompts as a transformative tool for enhancing reasoning in LLMs, providing actionable insights for the development of more capable and contextually aware AI systems.

The paper is structured as follows: Section [II](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S2 "II Conceptual Metaphor Theory ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") introduces CMT, followed by Section [III](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S3 "III CMT Prompting Approach ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models"), which details the CMT-based prompting approach. Section [IV](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S4 "IV Related Work ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") reviews related work, while Section [V](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S5 "V Benchmark Problems ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") presents the benchmark problems. Section [VI](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S6 "VI Experiments ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") describes the experimental setup and results. Finally, Section [VII](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S7 "VII Conclusion ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") discusses key findings and outlines future research directions.

## II Conceptual Metaphor Theory

CMT is a foundational concept in cognitive science that explores how humans understand and process abstract concepts through metaphoric mappings from more tangible, familiar domains. Introduced by George Lakoff and Mark Johnson [[1](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib1), [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib2)], CMT posits that metaphors are not merely linguistic devices but cognitive tools deeply embedded in human thought and reasoning. They enable individuals to comprehend complex or abstract ideas by linking them to more accessible and experiential domains. For example, the metaphor “time is money” frames the abstract concept of time using the concrete and familiar framework of financial transactions.

Central to CMT is the concept of source domains and target domains, which form the foundation of metaphorical thinking. The source domain represents a familiar, concrete concept grounded in physical experience or everyday life, such as spatial orientation, movement, or physical objects. In contrast, the target domain encapsulates an abstract, intangible idea that is often more challenging to conceptualize directly, such as emotions, time, morality, or complex systems. Through systematic mappings between these domains, specific properties, relationships, or structures from the source domain are transferred to the target domain. These mappings enable individuals to use their intuitive understanding of the source domain to make sense of abstract phenomena in the target domain.

For example, in the metaphor “time is a resource,” the source domain of resources (e.g., money or energy) lends its structure and relationships—such as scarcity, management, or expenditure—to the target domain of time. This allows people to conceptualize time as something that can be “spent,” “saved,” or “wasted,” making an abstract concept more tangible and actionable. Similarly, in the metaphor “knowledge is a journey,” the spatial and physical aspects of a journey—such as paths, destinations, obstacles, and progress—are mapped onto the process of acquiring knowledge, enabling individuals to conceptualize learning as a dynamic, goal-oriented activity.

These mappings are not arbitrary; they are shaped by embodied experiences and cultural context, which influence how individuals and societies construct and interpret metaphors. The systematic transfer of relationships and properties from source to target domains is what makes metaphors powerful tools for learning, problem-solving, and creativity. By framing unfamiliar or abstract concepts in terms of familiar ones, metaphors provide cognitive shortcuts that reduce complexity, aid memory, and enhance communication.

This central mechanism of CMT highlights the profound role of metaphor in shaping human thought, reasoning, and language. It also serves as the basis for applying CMT to computational systems, where metaphoric mappings can be leveraged to enhance the reasoning capabilities of artificial intelligence, particularly in tasks requiring abstraction and analogical thinking.

CMT has been extensively studied across disciplines, including linguistics, psychology, and education, as a tool for enhancing human learning and problem-solving. Its influence extends to computational applications, where metaphors have been used to structure knowledge representation and reasoning in artificial systems. However, its potential remains underexplored in the realm of large language models, despite their impressive capabilities in generating human-like text.

## III CMT Prompting Approach

The CMT prompting approach employs structured metaphorical reasoning through CoT prompting. This method enables LLMs to systematically interpret abstract concepts (target domains) by mapping them to familiar physical experiences (source domains). By structuring the reasoning process step by step, these models emulate human cognitive patterns, enhancing their ability to generate more insightful and contextually relevant responses. The approach is configured within Ollama models, with the configured versions referred to as CMT-Llama3.2, CMT-Phi3, CMT-Gemma, and CMT-Mistral.

### III-A LLM Configuration

The CMT prompting approach relies on three key components: source and target domains, inference through metaphorical mappings, and systematic reasoning. The system is guided by the instructions, see Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S3.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ III-A LLM Configuration ‣ III CMT Prompting Approach ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models").

### III-B Inference Through Source-Target Mappings

The CMT-prompted models leverage systematic source-target mappings to interpret and explain abstract concepts. This process involves identifying a relevant source domain, transferring its properties and relationships to the target domain, and constructing a coherent explanation or solution. These mappings, integrated as CMT-prompts in an LLM-configuration process, enable the models to internalize metaphorical reasoning and apply it dynamically across tasks.

This structured mapping process follows CoT-like approach within the CMT framework, guiding the model step-by-step through conceptual associations to derive logical inferences. By explicitly encoding the reasoning steps that bridge the source and target domains, CMT-prompted models develop a more structured interpretive process, much like CoT enables stepwise problem-solving in mathematical and logical tasks.

The following examples illustrate this structured mapping process, which is incorporated into the CMT prompt using a CoT-like approach, see Figure [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S3.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ III-B Inference Through Source-Target Mappings ‣ III CMT Prompting Approach ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models").

By embedding these examples in the CMT-prompts, the models acquire the ability to generalize structured metaphorical reasoning across a wide range of abstract and domain-specific tasks, enhancing their interpretive and explanatory capabilities.

### III-C Implementation Details

The CMT-prompted models operate with a balanced temperature parameter (temperature=0.7) to ensure a mix of creativity and coherence. The models are preconditioned with a system message that emphasizes their role as cognitive agents employing metaphorical reasoning.

The inclusion of these principles within the configuration process eliminates the need for manually crafting explicit CMT-based prompts for every task. Instead, the models are inherently capable of performing structured metaphorical reasoning, making them versatile for a wide range of applications, including problem-solving, teaching, and interpretation.

## IV Related Work

### IV-A Prompt Engineering

Prompt engineering has become a crucial technique for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Various strategies have been developed to improve model performance across different types of reasoning tasks. Zero-shot prompting generates responses without prior examples, relying on the model’s pre-trained knowledge, while few-shot prompting [[3](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib3)] improves accuracy by incorporating task-specific examples within the prompt. More structured approaches, such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting [[4](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib4)], further enhance reasoning by explicitly breaking down complex problems into sequential steps. Extending this idea, Tree of Thoughts (ToT) prompting [[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib5)] allows the model to explore multiple reasoning paths in parallel, improving decision-making in tasks that benefit from divergent thinking. Another related approach, ReAct [[6](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib6)], integrates logical reasoning with interactive decision-making, making it particularly useful for dynamic environments.

Beyond structured reasoning, several methods focus on optimizing prompts for improved performance. Prompt Breeder [[7](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib7)] applies evolutionary strategies to refine prompts iteratively, while Automated Prompt Engineering (APE) [[8](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib8)] and Optimization by PROmpting (OPRO) [[9](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib9)] automate the prompt design process through reinforcement learning and search-based techniques. These methods systematically fine-tune instructions to maximize the model’s effectiveness, reducing reliance on manual prompt crafting.

Cognitive Prompting [[10](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib10)] introduces structured cognitive operations to enhance problem-solving abilities in LLMs, incorporating step-by-step reasoning techniques to improve multi-step task performance. By guiding models through explicit reasoning structures, these approaches demonstrate notable improvements, particularly in arithmetic and logical reasoning benchmarks.

### IV-B LLMs and Conceptual Metaphor Theory

Recent work has explored prompting techniques to improve LLMs’ understanding of metaphors. Prystawski et al. [[11](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib11)] employ CoT-style prompts to guide models in analyzing metaphorical language, showing improved interpretative accuracy. Comsa et al. [[12](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib12)] introduce MiQA, a benchmark assessing LLMs’ ability to reason with conventional metaphors, combining metaphor detection with commonsense reasoning. Hicke and Kristensen-McLachlan [[13](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#bib.bib13)] evaluate models’ capabilities in systematically recognizing and annotating conceptual metaphors using linguistic annotation frameworks.

While these studies demonstrate progress in metaphor understanding, they primarily focus on recognition and classification rather than structured conceptual reasoning. This work builds on previous approaches by designing tasks that explicitly incorporate conceptual mappings, evaluating how well LLMs establish connections between source and target domains. By assessing models’ ability to apply metaphorical structures across diverse problem types, this study provides a more comprehensive evaluation of metaphor-driven reasoning in LLMs.

## V Benchmark Problems

To evaluate the ability of LLMs to comprehend, interpret, and reason using metaphorical language, we developed a benchmark comprising 100 problems. These problems are categorized into four distinct groups, each designed to assess a different facet of metaphor understanding, generation, and application. The benchmark examines both the recognition of existing metaphors and the capacity to construct novel conceptual mappings for abstract reasoning and explanation. Within each category, we created four specialized tasks to ensure a comprehensive evaluation.
To preserve the integrity of the benchmark, task instructions do not explicitly prompt the models to identify or rely on metaphorical reasoning.

### V-A Metaphor Identification and Mapping (MIM)

Metaphors play a fundamental role in human cognition, structuring abstract concepts through more concrete experiences.

Each task presents a sentence or passage in which a conceptual metaphor is embedded. The model must determine the key entities involved, recognize the source and target domains, and explain how the metaphor frames the underlying meaning. For instance, given the statement *“Their plan to revitalize the industry took root and began to grow stronger each year”*, an effective response should identify how *growth* in a biological sense is mapped onto the success of an economic or organizational strategy.

Performance is evaluated across three dimensions: (1) Precision in structural interpretation, assessing how well the response captures the conceptual elements of the metaphor; (2) Coherence of explanation, measuring logical consistency and insightfulness; and (3) Accuracy in mapping relationships, determining whether key relationships between entities are correctly identified. This structured evaluation allows for a fine-grained comparison between baseline LLMs and models enhanced with CMT-based reasoning strategies.

### V-B Domain-Specific Reasoning (DSR)

DSR tasks challenge the model to apply metaphorical reasoning in specialized fields, requiring precise conceptual mappings beyond conventional analogies. Unlike general metaphor identification tasks, these problems demand a structured explanatory approach, where the model must construct meaningful analogies that enhance comprehension of complex topics.

Each task presents an abstract or technical concept drawn from disciplines such as physics, biology, economics, artificial intelligence, and engineering. The model must provide an explanation that not only adheres to the principles of the domain but also introduces a novel, insightful metaphor that facilitates understanding. Traditional explanations, such as likening an electric circuit to water flowing through pipes, are insufficient. Instead, the model must generate alternative mappings that reveal deeper conceptual structures. For instance, explaining market competition should go beyond the common battlefield metaphor and explore alternative frameworks, such as ecological ecosystems or evolutionary dynamics.

The evaluation criteria for DSR tasks focus on three core aspects: (1) Precision in structural interpretation – assessing how well the response captures the key conceptual elements; (2) Coherence of explanation – evaluating the logical consistency and insightfulness of the account; and (3) Accuracy in mapping relationships – measuring the correct establishment of key relationships between described entities. These criteria ensure that responses are not only metaphorically rich but also technically sound.

### V-C Explanation and Teaching Tasks (ETT)

ETT evaluate a model’s ability to communicate complex concepts to a general audience using clear and intuitive analogies. These tasks focus on how effectively an AI system can translate specialized knowledge into accessible explanations that facilitate understanding. Instead of merely providing factual responses, the model must construct meaningful metaphors that enhance comprehension for non-expert readers.

The problem set includes a diverse range of domains, spanning artificial intelligence, economics, physics, biology, and social sciences. For instance, an ETT task may ask the model to explain how neural networks learn using an analogy, such as comparing them to a child learning through trial and error. Similarly, an economic concept like inflation may be illustrated through a metaphor likening it to air being pumped into a balloon, causing it to expand over time. The model’s explanations are assessed based on their clarity, conceptual fidelity, and the effectiveness of the chosen metaphor in conveying the underlying principles.

Each task is evaluated according to three core criteria: (1) Clarity for Non-Experts, which measures how well the response avoids unnecessary complexity while maintaining accuracy; (2) Conceptual Accuracy, assessing whether the explanation remains faithful to the fundamental ideas of the concept being described; and (3) Effectiveness of Analogy or Metaphor, which evaluates how well the chosen metaphor aids comprehension and aligns with the original concept.

### V-D Reading Comprehension of Metaphors (RCM)

RCM tasks assess a model’s ability to interpret metaphors in literary, journalistic, and conceptual texts. These tasks evaluate not only the recognition of metaphorical language but also the capacity to establish meaningful connections between source and target domains and explain how metaphors shape meaning and perception. The complexity of metaphor interpretation makes these tasks particularly useful for benchmarking conceptual understanding in large language models.

Each task presents a metaphorical passage, requiring the model to detect the metaphor, analyze the underlying conceptual mappings, and articulate its interpretative significance. For example, in the passage *“His words cut deep, leaving wounds that time struggled to heal,”* the model must recognize how the metaphor of physical injury conveys emotional pain and lasting psychological effects. Similarly, in *“The economy is a house of cards, ready to collapse at the slightest disturbance,”* the model must infer how the imagery of structural fragility conveys economic instability.

Performance in RCM tasks is evaluated based on three key criteria. The first is precision in metaphor identification, which measures the model’s ability to accurately detect metaphorical expressions and distinguish them from literal statements. The second is completeness of source-target mapping, assessing whether the model correctly links metaphorical source domains, such as fire, seeds, or storms, to their respective target domains, such as motivation, knowledge, or political unrest. Finally, depth of interpretive insight is used to evaluate how effectively the model explains the metaphor’s impact on meaning and perception, considering logical coherence and conceptual depth.

## VI Experiments

We evaluate the CMT-based prompting approach against baseline prompts across the four introduced distinct categories of tasks.

### VI-A Evaluation

Each task was evaluated based on three predefined criteria, with scores ranging from 1 to 5. Independent evaluations by multiple annotators ensured consistency, with inter-rater agreement assessed for reliability.

To systematically compare responses, we employed Llama3.3 (70B) as an expert evaluator. This model assessed both baseline and CMT responses against predefined criteria, including accuracy, coherence, and relevance.

The evaluator received detailed instructions, including the task description, baseline and CMT responses, and scoring criteria. It assigned scores from 1 to 5 for each criterion, provided justifications, and identified the superior response or noted ties. The evaluation prompt is shown in Figure [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S6.F3 "Figure 3 ‣ VI-A Evaluation ‣ VI Experiments ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models").

This structured approach ensures fairness and consistency while leveraging Llama3.3’s capabilities for insightful assessments of CMT’s impact.

### VI-B Experimental Setup

We selected representative tasks from each category. These tasks were tested across four large language models: Llama3.2 with 3B parameters, Phi3 with 3.8B parameters, Gemma2 with 2B parameters, and Mistral with 7B parameters. Each model was tested in both its baseline configuration and with CMT-enhanced prompting. The responses were evaluated based on the predefined criteria, and results were averaged across all tasks within each category.

### VI-C Experimental Results

Figure [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S6.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ VI-C Experimental Results ‣ VI Experiments ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") presents the average baseline- and CMT-LLM scores for the four LLMs in MIM, DSR, ETT, and RCM.

The results of MIM, see Figure [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S6.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ VI-C Experimental Results ‣ VI Experiments ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") (left), show that CMT-enhanced models consistently outperform baselines in metaphor interpretation. CMT prompting is particularly effective for complex conceptual mappings, such as economic and physical burden metaphors, enabling more precise source-target alignment. For instance, in ”The economy is entering a deep freeze,” CMT improved recognition of layered metaphorical structures. While baselines handled conventional expressions well, CMT enhanced explanatory depth, especially for nuanced metaphors. Models like Mistral and Phi3 showed the greatest improvements in coherence and interpretative insight. Metaphors involving movement, transformation, and containment saw the highest gains from CMT’s structured reasoning. Even where baselines performed well, CMT often provided additional conceptual clarity without adding unnecessary complexity. Minor anomalies, such as unusually high CMT-enhanced scores, suggest potential refinements to evaluation methods.

The evaluation of DSR, see Figure [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S6.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ VI-C Experimental Results ‣ VI Experiments ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") (2nd from left), shows that CMT-prompted models generally match or surpass baselines, with notable improvements in specific cases. CMT enhances clarity and structured reasoning, particularly in tasks requiring abstract conceptual mapping. For explanations of the immune system and blockchain operations, CMT provided richer metaphorical insights, aiding comprehension. Phi3, Gemma2, and Mistral frequently outperformed their baselines, especially in analogy-driven tasks. Financial markets, neural adaptation, and encryption showed significant gains, where metaphorical reasoning improved conceptual understanding. While Llama3.2 exhibited some fluctuations, most models benefited from CMT in structuring domain-specific explanations. CMT’s ability to foster intuitive reasoning is particularly valuable in technical subjects that rely on conceptual transfer. Mixed results in economic inflation and competition dynamics suggest room for refining metaphor application.

The evaluation of ETT, see Figure [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S6.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ VI-C Experimental Results ‣ VI Experiments ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") (2nd from right), highlights CMT’s effectiveness in improving clarity and comprehension. CMT-enhanced models consistently outperformed or matched baselines, particularly in tasks requiring conceptual mapping and intuitive explanations. Significant gains were observed in explaining neural networks, historical alliances, and artificial intelligence, where metaphorical reasoning provided an accessible framework. Llama3.2 and Phi3 showed notable improvements under CMT, demonstrating its value in structuring explanations. Metaphor-enhanced prompting simplified complex topics like economic inflation and stock markets, enhancing accessibility. While some tasks saw similar performance between baseline and CMT models, CMT generally facilitated more coherent and engaging explanations. Physics and biology particularly benefited from structured analogies, aiding conceptual transfer. Relating abstract principles to familiar experiences proved advantageous for teaching-oriented outputs. These findings reinforce CMT as a powerful framework for enhancing language model explanations.

The RCM evaluation, see Figure [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01901v1#S6.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ VI-C Experimental Results ‣ VI Experiments ‣ Conceptual Metaphor Theory as a Prompting Paradigm for Large Language Models") (right), demonstrates CMT’s effectiveness in metaphor interpretation and figurative language analysis. CMT-enhanced models consistently outperformed or matched baselines across various metaphorical contexts. Llama3.2, Gemma2, and Mistral showed substantial gains in understanding complex metaphors when guided by CMT-based reasoning. Shakespeare’s “All the world’s a stage” and similar constructs particularly benefited from CMT’s structured approach to meaning extraction. CMT-enhanced models exhibited deeper comprehension of connotations and abstract associations, improving metaphor interpretation. In political and economic metaphors, CMT models provided more nuanced and contextually appropriate readings. Phi3 demonstrated strong improvements, highlighting CMT’s adaptability to different linguistic frameworks. While some results showed parity between baseline and CMT models, CMT generally facilitated stronger metaphor deconstruction and contextual reasoning. Structured conceptual mappings enriched interpretation, yielding more insightful analyses.

## VII Conclusion

Among individual models, Llama3.2 benefits the most from CMT, showing strong improvements across ETT, DSR, and parts of RCM, reinforcing its ability to generate structured responses. Phi3 displays significant gains in ETT and moderate improvements in RCM, suggesting CMT enhances conceptual mapping in this model. Gemma2 performs well in structured reasoning but faces challenges in metaphor-related tasks, particularly in RCM, where its scores remain inconsistent. Mistral demonstrates the most unpredictable performance—while it benefits in DSR and some ETT cases, its RCM performance remains inconsistent, and MIM shows limited gains. These findings indicate that CMT is particularly impactful for tasks requiring structured explanations, conceptual abstraction, and cognitive organization, reinforcing its potential as a powerful prompting strategy for enhancing LLM capabilities in reasoning and teaching-based tasks. Furthermore, the varying degrees of improvement across models suggest that CMT’s effectiveness depends not only on the nature of the task but also on the underlying architecture and training dynamics of each LLM.

## VIII Outlook

Future work will explore extending the CMT framework to real-world, multimodal tasks and evaluating its applicability to emerging challenges in explainability, reasoning, and collaborative AI systems.
Expanding the benchmark dataset to include more complex real-world tasks, such as multi-turn dialogue or cross-domain reasoning, could provide deeper insights into the scalability of CMT pro

--- SOURCE 3: [PDF] Exploration of Conceptual Metaphor Theory in Cognitive Linguistics ---
URL: https://www.clausiuspress.com/assets/default/article/2024/01/30/article_1706671340.pdf

SUMMARY:
Exploration of Conceptual Metaphor Theory in Cognitive Linguistics Qingkun Lin College of Foreign Languages, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan, Hebei, 063210, China Keywords: Conceptual metaphor theory; Cognitive linguistics; Love is a Journey; Cognitive processes Abstract: Conceptual metaphor theory posits that metaphors are cognitive phenomena shaping our understanding of the world, not just linguistic tools. They found that people use journey-related language and expressions to talk about love, such as “we’ve come a long way” or “we’re at a crossroads in our relationship.” This metaphorical concept influences how we perceive and experience love, shaping our expectations, understanding of obstacles, and the sense of progression in relationships. This metaphorical concept involves mapping the domain of love onto the domain of a journey, where the characteristics, experiences, and dynamics associated with a physical journey are used to understand and conceptualize love [6].

FULL CONTENT:
Exploration of Conceptual Metaphor Theory in Cognitive Linguistics Qingkun Lin College of Foreign Languages, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan, Hebei, 063210, China Keywords: Conceptual metaphor theory; Cognitive linguistics; Love is a Journey; Cognitive processes Abstract: Conceptual metaphor theory posits that metaphors are cognitive phenomena shaping our understanding of the world, not just linguistic tools. This essay explores the metaphor "Love is a Journey" within this framework, analyzing its grounding in embodied experiences and its impact on understanding love. Like mapping, projection, embodied simulation, and conceptual integration play key roles in shaping language, thought, and perception. While the theory contributes significantly, critics challenge its universality and call for more empirical support. Counterarguments suggest alternative perspectives on love, such as an existential state or a state of being, and emphasize cultural and personal variations. Recognizing these perspectives enriches our understanding of metaphorical concepts in the realm of romantic relationships, highlighting the complexity and diversity of human experiences. 1. Introduction Conceptual metaphor theory is a fundamental theory in cognitive linguistics proposed by George Lakoff and Mark Johnson [1]. It suggests that metaphor is not merely a linguistic device used for poetic or rhetorical purposes, but rather a cognitive phenomenon that shapes our understanding of the world. According to this theory, our conceptual system is grounded in metaphorical thinking, where abstract or complex ideas are understood and structured in terms of more concrete and embodied experiences. By studying conceptual metaphors, cognitive linguists gain insights into how our thoughts and language are interconnected. This theory has numerous implications for various fields, including linguistics, psychology, philosophy, and communication studies. It helps us understand how metaphoric language influences our perception, reasoning, and communication, shedding light on how our cognitive processes are shaped by cultural and experiential factors. Conceptual metaphor theory is significant in cognitive linguistics as it reveals the central role of metaphor in our cognitive processes, demonstrating how our conceptual systems are structured by metaphoric thinking. By investigating the relationship between language and thought, this theory provides valuable insights into how our minds make sense of the world and construct meaning. The purpose of this essay is to explore the metaphorical concept “Love is a journey” within the framework of conceptual metaphor theory in cognitive linguistics. The aim is to analyze how this Lecture Notes on Language and Literature (2024) Clausius Scientific Press, Canada DOI: 10.23977/langl.2024.070113 ISSN 2523-5869 Vol. 7 Num. 1 80 metaphorical concept structures our understanding of love, shapes our language use, and influences our cognitive processes. 2. Background on Conceptual Metaphor Theory 2.1 Main Principles and Assumptions Conceptual metaphor theory is based on several key principles and assumptions that underpin its analysis of metaphorical thinking. Here are the main principles and assumptions of conceptual metaphor theory: First of all, conceptual metaphor theory suggests that metaphorical thinking involves the mapping of concepts from one domain (source domain) onto another (target domain). The source domain, which is more concrete and well-understood, provides the basis for understanding the target domain, which is often abstract or complex. For example, in “Love is a Journey,” the source domain is the domain of physical travel or movement, and the target domain is the abstract concept of love. Furthermore, it posits that metaphorical mappings are not arbitrary [2] but rather rooted in universal human experiences and embodied cognition, which assumes that certain metaphorical concepts are shared across cultures, reflecting common patterns of human thought and experience. For example, the metaphorical concept “Time is money” is found in various languages and cultures, highlighting a shared understanding of time as a valuable resource. Embodiment asserts that our bodily experiences and sensorimotor interactions with the world shape our understanding of abstract concepts. Conceptual metaphors are grounded in our bodily experiences and perception, allowing us to comprehend abstract ideas through concrete and embodied frames. For instance, the metaphor “Happy is up, sad is down” reflects our physical experiences of looking up when we feel joyful and feeling down when we are sad. Conceptual metaphor theory suggests that metaphorical mappings are guided by cognitive constraints that structure our conceptual systems. These constraints help determine which metaphors are conventional and salient in a given culture or language. The choice of metaphors is influenced by factors such as cultural norms, cognitive ease, and communicative efficiency. It also emphasizes the role of experiential and cultural factors in shaping metaphoric concepts. Metaphors are not seen as arbitrary linguistic expressions but as reflections of our experiences, values, and cultural contexts. Different cultures may have unique metaphorical concepts that reflect their specific experiences and cultural frames of reference. Overall, these principles and assumptions of conceptual metaphor theory provide a framework for understanding how metaphorical thinking operates, how it shapes our language and thought, and how it is deeply rooted in our embodied and cultural experiences. They allow researchers to analyze how metaphorical concepts structure our understanding of abstract domains and contribute to our cognitive processes. 2.2 The Cognitive Processes Involved Conceptual metaphor theory proposes that metaphor is not just a linguistic device but a cognitive phenomenon that plays a crucial role in shaping our language and thought. The theory suggests that metaphorical thinking involves several cognitive processes and has profound implications for how we understand and conceptualize the world. Here's a discussion of the cognitive processes involved in conceptual metaphor and its role in shaping language and thought: 81 2.2.1 Mapping and Projection Conceptual metaphor involves mapping concepts from a more concrete and well-understood source domain onto a less concrete target domain. This mapping allows us to understand the abstract or complex target domain in terms of the more familiar source domain. For example, in the metaphor “Love is a journey,” we project the characteristics and experiences associated with a physical journey onto the concept of love. This mapping and projection facilitate our understanding of love by providing a conceptual framework that we can relate to and comprehend. 2.2.2 Embodied Simulation Conceptual metaphor theory emphasizes the role of embodied cognition in metaphorical thinking. Our bodily experiences and sensorimotor interactions with the world shape our understanding of abstract concepts. When we encounter a metaphor, our minds simulate the experiential and sensory aspects of the source domain, allowing us to make connections and derive meaning in the target domain. For example, when we talk about “grasping an idea” or “feeling heavy-hearted,” we activate the sensory and motor experiences associated with physical grasping and physical weight. 2.2.3 Conceptual Integration Conceptual metaphors involve integrating information from both the source and target domains to create a coherent understanding [3]. This process of conceptual integration allows us to connect and blend the characteristics, associations, and experiences of the source domain with the target domain [4]. It enables us to make inferences, reason, and create new insights based on metaphorical mapping. For example, when we use “Time is money,” we integrate the concepts of time and money allowing us to reason about time in terms of value, scarcity and investment. 2.2.4 Influence on Language and Thought Metaphor is not limited to language alone but influences our thought processes and conceptualizations. Metaphorical language reflects our metaphorical thinking, and by using metaphors, we shape and communicate our understanding of abstract concepts. Metaphors can guide our thinking, influence our judgments and decisions, and shape our cultural and social perspectives. They play a crucial role in communication, enabling us to convey complex ideas and evoke vivid imagery. In summary, the cognitive processes involved in conceptual metaphor include mapping, projection, embodied simulation, conceptual integration, and conceptual structuring. Through these processes, metaphor shapes our language use, influences our thought patterns, structures our conceptual systems, and provides us with powerful tools for understanding and communicating abstract concepts. 2.3 Examples of Conceptual Metaphor Theory 2.3.1 Love is a Journey In this metaphorical concept, love is understood and expressed in terms of a journey. Lakoff and Johnson's original work on conceptual metaphor theory explored this metaphor extensively. They found that people use journey-related language and expressions to talk about love, such as “we’ve come a long way” or “we’re at a crossroads in our relationship.” This metaphorical concept influences how we perceive and experience love, shaping our expectations, understanding of obstacles, and the sense of progression in relationships. 82 2.3.2 Argument is War This metaphorical concept conceptualizes arguments as battles or wars. Studies have shown that people often use war-related language in argumentative discourse, such as “attack,” “defend,” or “win an argument.” This metaphor influences how we approach and engage in arguments, framing them as competitive encounters where we aim to defeat opponents. The metaphor also shapes our reasoning strategies, where we employ tactics and strive for victory. 2.3.3 Time is Money The metaphorical concept of “Time is money” is widely recognized and has been extensively studied. This metaphor influences our understanding of time as a valuable resource that can be wasted, spent, saved [5], or invested. It shapes our perception of time as a limited commodity and influences our attitudes towards punctuality, efficiency, and productivity. Studies have explored how this metaphor affects our time management behaviours and decision-making processes. These are just a few examples that demonstrate how conceptual metaphor theory has been applied to various domains of language and thought. Researchers have explored numerous metaphorical concepts, examining their impact on perception, reasoning, emotions, and cultural understanding. These studies highlight the versatility and significance of metaphorical thinking in shaping our cognitive processes and language use. 3. Analysis of the Metaphorical Concept 3.1 Explanation of Love is a journey The specific metaphorical concept chosen for analysis is “Love is a Journey”. This metaphorical concept involves mapping the domain of love onto the domain of a journey, where the characteristics, experiences, and dynamics associated with a physical journey are used to understand and conceptualize love [6]. In this metaphor, the source domain is the concept of a journey, which is more concrete and well-understood. The target domain is the abstract concept of love, which can be complex and subjective. By mapping the source domain onto the target domain, we project the qualities, experiences, and stages of a journey onto our understanding of love. This metaphorical concept suggests that love is seen as a process or a transformative experience that involves movement, progression, and exploration. It implies that love has a beginning, a middle, and an end, and can involve obstacles, detours, and discoveries along the way. It also suggests that love requires effort, commitment, and navigation, much like embarking on a physical journey. The metaphorical concept “Love is a Journey” provides a framework for understanding and discussing romantic relationships. It influences how we talk about love, the language and expressions we use to describe our experiences, and the expectations we have for the course of a relationship. This metaphorical concept shapes our understanding of love by providing a structured and relatable framework that helps us make sense of the complexities and dynamics of romantic relationships. 3.2 The Grounding and Shaping of Our Understanding The metaphorical concept “Love is a Journey” is grounded in embodied experiences and shapes our understanding of the target domain of love. Here's an analysis of how this metaphorical concept is rooted in our embodied experiences and influences our comprehension of love: 83 3.2.1 Embodied Experiences The metaphorical concept draws upon our embodied experiences of physical journeys. As human beings, we have a rich and varied history of engaging in actual journeys, whether it's travelling to new places, exploring unfamiliar terrain, or experiencing the ups and downs of a voyage. These embodied experiences provide a basis for understanding and conceptualizing abstract concepts like love by mapping the qualities, stages, and dynamics of a journey into our understanding of love. 3.2.2 Progression and Direction The metaphorical concept of a journey implies a sense of progression and direction in love. Just as a physical journey involves moving from one place to another, love is seen as a process that evolves over time. It suggests that love has a beginning, a middle, and potentially an end. We understand love as moving forward or backwards, advancing or regressing, similar to how we perceive movement and progress in a journey. 3.2.3 Obstacles and Challenges Journeys often involve obstacles, detours, and challenges, and this is reflected in the metaphorical concept of love. Love is understood as encountering obstacles and overcoming them, navigating through difficulties, and finding a way forward. The metaphor allows us to comprehend the challenges that can arise in relationships and provides a framework for understanding how to address and overcome them. 3.2.4 Effort and Commitment Just as a physical journey requires effort and commitment, the metaphorical concept suggests that love also requires these qualities. Love is not seen as a passive experience but as an active endeavour that demands dedication, perseverance, and investment. The metaphor highlights the idea that successful relationships require ongoing effort and commitment, much like the commitment required to undertake and complete a journey. 3.2.5 Destination and Transformation: Journeys often have destinations, and they can be transformative experiences. Similarly, the metaphorical concept of love as a journey suggests that love can lead to a destination or a desired outcome. It implies that love can bring about personal growth, change, and transformation. The metaphor captures the idea that love has the potential to take us on a transformative path and shape our lives in meaningful ways. In summary, the metaphorical concept “Love is a Journey” is grounded in our embodied experiences of physical journeys. It draws upon our understanding of progression, direction, obstacles, effort, commitment, and transformation in actual journeys and applies these concepts to our comprehension of love. This metaphor shapes our understanding of love by providing a structured framework that allows us to make sense of the complexities, dynamics, and experiences associated with romantic relationships. 4. Potential Criticisms or Limitations Conceptual metaphor theory has received both acclaim and criticism within the field of cognitive linguistics [7]. While it has made significant contributions to our understanding of how metaphors shape language and thought, there are also several criticisms and limitations worth considering. 84 Critics of conceptual metaphor theory assert that its assumption of universality in metaphorical mappings across languages and cultures is flawed. They argue that metaphors can vary significantly in different cultural and linguistic contexts, challenging the theory's claim of cross-cultural applicability. Moreover, the theory’s Western-centric focus raises concerns about neglecting diverse metaphorical systems in other cultural and linguistic settings. Critics contend that conceptual metaphor theory disproportionately emphasizes the structural mapping of source domains onto target domains, sidelining other crucial cognitive processes involved in metaphor comprehension, such as image schemas or embodied simulation. While the theory offers valuable insights, detractors insist it lacks robust empirical support, relying heavily on linguistic analysis and introspective judgments rather than rigorous experimental methodologies. They advocate for more empirical research to substantiate the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying conceptual metaphors. Additionally, some argue that the theory's reductionist perspective oversimplifies complex cognitive processes, failing to capture the entirety of human thought within a broader conceptual framework. 5. Counterarguments or Alternative Perspectives Related While the metaphorical concept “Love is a Journey” is widely used and has been studied extensively, there are alternative perspectives and counterarguments that challenge its validity and applicability. Here are some counterarguments and alternative perspectives related to this metaphorical concept: 5.1 Love as an Existential State Some argue that the metaphorical understanding of love as a journey may limit our perception of love by emphasizing its temporal and dynamic aspects. An alternative perspective suggests that love should be conceptualized as an existential state or a fundamental aspect of human existence, rather than a process or progression. This perspective emphasizes the depth, permanence, and transformative nature of love, rather than its temporal dynamics. 5.2 Love as a State of Being Another alternative perspective proposes that love should be understood as a state of being rather than a journey. This perspective focuses on the emotional and experiential aspects of love, emphasizing a sense of connection, fulfilment, and shared experiences in the present moment. It suggests that love is not necessarily defined by progress, obstacles, or a linear trajectory, but by the quality of connection and emotional experience in the here and now. 5.3 Love as a Destination The metaphor “Love is a Journey” also implies that love is an ongoing process with an endpoint or a final destination. However, some argue that love should be seen as a destination in itself rather than a journey towards a specific goal. This perspective emphasizes the value of love as a state of fulfilment and contentment rather than viewing it as a means to achieve personal growth or milestones. 5.4 Cultural and Personal Variations The consideration of cultural variations in the conceptualization of love is emphasized in the 85 provided text. Different cultures employ alternative metaphors or conceptual frameworks, such as viewing love as a garden, a dance, or a symphony. These metaphors offer diverse perspectives on love, underscoring the influence of cultural context on metaphoric concepts. Additionally, individuals may possess varied metaphorical frameworks and personal experiences that shape their understanding of love. Although the journey metaphor is widely resonant, it may not fully encompass the diverse range of individual experiences and interpretations. Some individuals may find alignment with alternative metaphors or conceptualizations that better reflect their unique perspectives and personal journeys. While the metaphorical concept of “Love is a Journey” has been widely embraced and studied, it is important to recognize alternative perspectives and counterarguments. These perspectives highlight alternative metaphors, emphasize the experiential and existential aspects of love, and acknowledge the influence of cultural and personal variations. Considering these alternative perspectives enriches our understanding of love and encourages a more nuanced exploration of metaphoric concepts in relation to romantic relationships. 6. Conclusion Conceptual metaphor theory is a pivotal framework within cognitive linguistics that illuminates how metaphorical thinking shapes language, thought, and cognition. By mapping abstract concepts onto concrete domains, this theory reveals the embodiment of our cognitive processes and highlights the systematic nature of metaphorical understanding. Through its insights in various fields, conceptual metaphor theory has significantly advanced our understanding of how metaphorical concepts influence our perception, reasoning, communication, and social interactions. Ultimately, this theory deepens our appreciation for the intricate relationship between language, cognition, and embodied experiences. References [1] Lakoff, G., & Johnson, M. (1980). Metaphors We Live By. Chicago: University of Chicago Press. [2] Jiang Jing. (2008). The Impact of Source Language Concepts in Metaphorization. Fudan University. [3] Brandt, L. (2013). Metaphor and the Communicative Mind [J]. SSRN Electronic Journal. [4] Pu, J., & Fang, H. (2020). A Study of Conceptual Metaphors in Economic Reports. US-China Foreign Language, 18(5): 5. [5] Liping Zhang. (2022). Research on the Construction of the Meaning of Shandong Image on the Cover of Towards the World from a Multimodal Metaphor Perspective. Liaoning University. [6] Kovecses Z. (2010). Metaphor: A Practical Introduction. A Practical Introduction. [7] Xin Zhang. (2021). Development and Critiques of Conceptual Metaphor Theory. Conceptual metaphor theory revolutionized our understanding of language and cognition. It proposes that we use concrete experiences to understand abstract ideas, shaping our thoughts and everyday language . This theory challenges traditional views of metaphor as merely a linguistic device.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Metaphors shape our cognitive processes, allowing us to frame abstract concepts in terms of familiar experiences, enhancing clarity and depth of comprehension.
Concrete mappings provide relatable frameworks to interpret complex ideas, enabling easier grasp of abstract phenomena through familiar, tangible experiences.
Cultural contexts significantly impact metaphorical expressions, reflecting shared values and experiences, which shape how communities interpret and utilize metaphors.
Metaphors vary across languages, illustrating diverse conceptual frameworks, such as 'love is a journey,' showcasing cultural nuances in understanding relationships.
Recognizing metaphors in education can enhance teaching strategies, facilitating deeper learning by linking new information to students' existing knowledge through metaphorical reasoning.
